title: Nigeria halts Christian pilgrimages to Holy Land over Middle East conflict

originalTitle: While US encourages Kurds to attack Iran, history serves darker warning
title: Friends Far Away: The Kurds and Big Choices!
content:
Hello from Africa, My Amazing Friends!

Hello, hello, hello! It's your friendly reporter, Kojo, here, bringing you super exciting news from around our big, beautiful world! Today, we're going to learn about some special people who live very, very far away from us, but who are just as important. They are called the Kurds! They have a big decision to think about, and we're going to understand it together, step by step, like we're solving a fun puzzle.

Who are the Kurds?

Imagine a group of people who are like a big family. They have their own special language, their own beautiful songs, and their own yummy food! That's a bit like the Kurds. They are an ancient group of people, which means they have been living in their homes for a very, very long time. They love their land and their culture. Did you know? The Kurds have their own special new year celebration called Newroz! It's a time for picnics, dancing, and lighting big bonfires. Sounds like fun, doesn't it?

Where Do the Kurds Live?

Now, imagine our wonderful continent of Africa. The Kurds live in a place called the Middle East, which is not too far from Africa, across the big ocean. But here’s something interesting: they don't have their *own* country! They live in parts of many different countries. These countries are called Turkey, Iraq, Syria, and Iran. So, they are spread out like sprinkles on a big cake! Sometimes, it's hard for them because they wish they could all live together in their *own* country. But for now, they are brave and strong, living in different places.

Meet Iran, a Neighbour

One of the countries where many Kurds live, and where they have a lot of family, is near a big country called Iran. Iran is a country with lots of history, beautiful buildings, and busy cities. It's like a big house next door to where some Kurdish families live. Just like we have neighbours, the Kurds have Iran as a neighbour. Did you know? Iran is famous for its beautiful, colourful carpets! People work very hard to weave amazing patterns into them, and they are like big pieces of art for your floor!

A Big Country Far Away: America

Now, let's think about a country that is very, very far away, across another big ocean. This country is called America, or sometimes people call it the United States. It's a very big and powerful country, like a giant elephant that can do many things. America often talks to many countries and groups of people all over the world, trying to help or make things better. Did you know? America is so big that if you wanted to drive from one side to the other, it would take you many days and nights! It's home to many different kinds of people and cultures, just like our Africa!

What Are People Talking About?

So, here's where it gets interesting! America is talking to the Kurds. Imagine a big friend (America) talking to another friend (the Kurds) and saying, "Hey, maybe you could try to make some changes in that neighbour's house (Iran)? Maybe you could make things better for yourselves there?" It's like if your friend told you to ask your neighbour to change something in their garden. It's a very serious thing to talk about, because it might cause a lot of worry or even trouble. America is suggesting that the Kurds might want to try and stand up for themselves or ask for more freedom and rights in the part of Iran where they live.

Remembering the Past

Now, the Kurds are thinking very, very carefully about what America is saying. Why are they thinking so hard? Because of things that happened in the past. Imagine if your friend promised to share their favourite toy with you, but then they didn't. You would feel sad, wouldn't you? And next time, you would think, "Will my friend really share this time?" Well, something like that happened to the Kurds. In the past, sometimes big countries promised to help them, but then, for different reasons, they couldn't or didn't. This made the Kurds feel like they were left alone. It was a bit like a promise that was broken, and that can make people feel very careful about trusting again. Did you know? The Kurdish people have a very long history of being brave and strong. They have faced many challenges but always try to keep their culture and spirit alive!

Thinking About Tomorrow

So, the Kurds are looking at America's suggestion, and they are thinking: "If we try to make these changes, will it really help us? Will we be safer and happier? Or will it make things even harder?" They are thinking about their children, their families, and their future. They want peace and a good life, just like you and me. They are trying to decide what is the best thing for *them* right now, and for all the Kurdish families. It's a very big decision, and they want to make sure they choose the path that will truly bring them joy and safety, not more sadness or trouble. It's like choosing between two paths in a forest. One path looks exciting but might have bumps. The other path looks safe but maybe not as exciting. The Kurds are looking at both paths and thinking hard about which one is best for their big family.

Being Kind and Making Choices

So, my wonderful friends, what can we learn from this? We learn that people all over the world, even far away like the Kurds, want to be safe and happy. We also learn that making big choices is hard, especially when you remember things that happened before. It’s always important to be kind to our neighbours and to think carefully before we make big decisions. And when we promise something, we should always try our very best to keep that promise. The Kurds are hoping for a future filled with peace and happiness. Let's send them our good thoughts and hopes! This is Kojo, your friendly reporter, signing off! See you next time for more amazing stories from our world!

title: Previously silent Iran players sing and salute anthem
